evals.name: cooperative-pool-simple
evals.expected_output: semaphore.wait() blocks a cooperative thread. The cooperative pool is capped at CPU core count (4-10 on iPhones). Under load all threads can be waiting, starving the pool and causing deadlock. Fix: use withCheckedThrowingContinuation to avoid blocking.

assertions.text: Identifies semaphore.wait() blocks the cooperative thread pool
assertions.rationale: Core anti-pattern from crash-patterns.md

assertions.text: Mentions the cooperative pool is capped at CPU core count (4-10 threads on iPhones)
assertions.rationale: Specific pool constraint from skill — baseline typically omits the thread cap number

assertions.text: Recommends withCheckedContinuation or withCheckedThrowingContinuation as the fix
assertions.rationale: Skill's prescribed fix over Unsafe continuation

evals.name: cooperative-pool-medium
evals.expected_output: Two blocking calls in an async context: (1) Thread.sleep blocks the cooperative thread — replace with Task.sleep(for:). (2) String(contentsOf:) is synchronous blocking file I/O that stalls a cooperative thread — wrap in a DispatchQueue + withCheckedContinuation or use an async file API to move the blocking work off the cooperative pool.

assertions.text: Identifies Thread.sleep blocks the cooperative thread (should use Task.sleep)
assertions.rationale: Common blocking call inside async function

assertions.text: Identifies String(contentsOf:) is synchronous blocking I/O in an async context
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly lists sync file I/O as a cooperative pool blocker — baseline often misses this

assertions.text: Recommends wrapping the blocking I/O in a DispatchQueue + continuation or using an async file API
assertions.rationale: Correct fix for blocking I/O in async context from skill

assertions.text: Mentions LIBDISPATCH_COOPERATIVE_POOL_STRICT=1 environment variable for detecting cooperative pool blocking in tests
assertions.rationale: Skill-specific debugging tool — WITH response mentions this, WITHOUT does not

assertions.text: Recommends Clock protocol injection (e.g., ContinuousClock, TestClock) for testable sleep replacement
assertions.rationale: WITH response provides Clock protocol testability pattern; WITHOUT only suggests Task.sleep without testability

evals.name: cooperative-pool-complex
evals.expected_output: Two compounding issues: (1) Data(contentsOf:) is synchronous blocking I/O — all 500 child tasks immediately block their cooperative threads, exhausting the pool and triggering watchdog. (2) All 500 tasks are added without throttling — should limit concurrent tasks to ProcessInfo.processInfo.activeProcessorCount. Fix: use URLSession async API or wrap Data(contentsOf:) in a DispatchQueue + continuation, and throttle the TaskGroup so only N tasks run at once.

assertions.text: Identifies Data(contentsOf:) is blocking I/O inside TaskGroup child tasks
assertions.rationale: Compound issue: blocking I/O inside TaskGroup — skill covers this specifically

assertions.text: Identifies 500 unbounded tasks cause cooperative pool exhaustion and watchdog kills
assertions.rationale: Unbounded TaskGroup growth is a crash pattern from skill

assertions.text: Recommends throttling child tasks to ProcessInfo.processInfo.activeProcessorCount
assertions.rationale: Skill's specific throttle target — baseline may say 'limit tasks' without the exact API

assertions.text: Recommends URLSession async API or DispatchQueue + continuation wrapper for file I/O
assertions.rationale: Correct fix for blocking I/O inside cooperative pool

assertions.text: Mentions LIBDISPATCH_COOPERATIVE_POOL_STRICT=1 to expose cooperative pool blocking in tests
assertions.rationale: WITH response includes this testing environment variable; WITHOUT does not mention it

evals.name: actor-isolation-simple
evals.expected_output: Actor state cannot be accessed synchronously from outside. Both store.userCount += 1 and store.currentUser?.name require await — the actor serializes access across its isolation boundary. Callers must use await store.userCount, or better, define mutation methods on the actor.

assertions.text: Identifies that actor state requires await from outside the actor
assertions.rationale: Fundamental actor isolation rule

assertions.text: Notes that both read (currentUser) and read-modify-write (userCount += 1) need await
assertions.rationale: Common misconception that reads are free — they are not

evals.name: actor-isolation-medium
evals.expected_output: Actor reentrancy: at the await suspension point (URLSession.shared.data), another caller can enter data(for:) for the same URL, find no cached value, and start a second fetch. Both callers download the same URL in parallel. Fix: use in-flight Task coalescing — store a Task<Data, Error> per URL in a separate dictionary. New callers await the existing task instead of starting a new fetch.

assertions.text: Identifies actor reentrancy at the await suspension point causes duplicate fetches
assertions.rationale: Core actor reentrancy concept from skill

assertions.text: Recommends in-flight Task coalescing using a [URL: Task<Data, Error>] dictionary
assertions.rationale: Specific coalescing pattern from actor-isolation.md — baseline may say 'add a flag' which is insufficient

evals.name: actor-isolation-complex
evals.expected_output: Three isolation issues: (1) Task.detached strips caller isolation — the closure runs nonisolated, so assigning self.items (a @MainActor property) from a detached task is a Swift 6 isolation crossing. Use Task {} instead to inherit @MainActor. (2) deinit is always nonisolated — accessing backgroundTask (a @MainActor-isolated property) from deinit is unsafe in Swift 6. Fix: add an explicit cleanup() method annotated @MainActor, call it from .onDisappear. (3) For deinit cleanup in Swift 6.1+, isolated deinit is available; before that, store a weak reference or use a nonisolated(unsafe) var with careful documentation.

assertions.text: Identifies Task.detached strips @MainActor isolation — should use Task {} to inherit caller's actor
assertions.rationale: Task.detached vs Task {} isolation distinction is a key skill rule

assertions.text: Identifies deinit is nonisolated and accessing @MainActor-isolated property from deinit is unsafe in Swift 6
assertions.rationale: Nonisolated deinit is explicitly covered in actor-isolation.md — baseline often misses it

assertions.text: Recommends explicit cleanup() method called from .onDisappear as the fix for deinit isolation
assertions.rationale: Skill's prescribed fix for @MainActor deinit access

evals.name: sendable-simple
evals.expected_output: @unchecked Sendable with no synchronization means the compiler trusts you but provides no protection — concurrent calls to updateTokens and reads of authToken/refreshToken are a data race at runtime. Fix: use actor (async access) or Mutex<State> / NSLock for synchronous access. Never use @unchecked Sendable without explicit synchronization.

assertions.text: Identifies @unchecked Sendable with no synchronization creates a runtime data race
assertions.rationale: Core @unchecked Sendable danger from sendable-transfer.md

assertions.text: Recommends actor or Mutex/NSLock as the fix
assertions.rationale: Skill prescribes these as safe alternatives

assertions.text: Notes the compiler trusts @unchecked Sendable — no safety guarantee at compile time
assertions.rationale: Key distinction: compiler trusts the annotation, runtime has no protection — baseline may miss this framing

evals.name: sendable-medium
evals.expected_output: AnalyticsService is a non-Sendable reference type. Task {} captures self and sends it across an isolation boundary — Swift 6 requires the captured value to be Sendable. Additionally, self.eventQueue.append(event) creates a data race: the Task runs concurrently with any other caller. Fix options: (1) Convert to actor (safest), (2) Add @MainActor isolation if this is a UI-adjacent service, (3) Use Mutex<[AnalyticsEvent]> to protect eventQueue if staying as a class.

assertions.text: Identifies AnalyticsService is non-Sendable and cannot be safely captured across isolation boundaries
assertions.rationale: Non-Sendable class in Task is a Swift 6 error

assertions.text: Identifies eventQueue.append from Task creates a data race with concurrent callers
assertions.rationale: Mutable state accessed from concurrent context

assertions.text: Recommends actor as the primary fix, or @MainActor / Mutex as alternatives
assertions.rationale: Skill provides a decision tree for isolation choice — baseline may only suggest one option

evals.name: sendable-complex
evals.expected_output: Two issues: (1) RequestConfig is a mutable class — the compiler does not infer Sendable for public types across module boundaries even if all stored properties happen to be Sendable. When config crosses the actor boundary into NetworkClient.perform(), Swift 6 requires it to be Sendable. Fix: make RequestConfig a struct (value type, implicitly Sendable once all stored properties are Sendable for public API, explicit Sendable conformance still recommended). (2) Alternative: add sending parameter to perform(_ config: sending RequestConfig) — allows non-Sendable types to transfer ownership across the boundary for that one call. Choosing between struct and sending depends on whether mutation after creation is needed.

assertions.text: Explains compiler does not infer Sendable for public types across module boundaries
assertions.rationale: Public type inference gap from sendable-transfer.md — commonly misunderstood

assertions.text: Identifies mutable class crossing actor boundary requires Sendable conformance
assertions.rationale: Core cross-isolation Sendable requirement

assertions.text: Recommends converting to struct as the primary fix
assertions.rationale: Value types are implicitly Sendable when properties are Sendable

assertions.text: Mentions sending parameter as an alternative for transferring ownership of non-Sendable types
assertions.rationale: sending keyword from sendable-transfer.md — baseline rarely knows this Swift 6 escape hatch

evals.name: asyncstream-simple
evals.expected_output: Three issues: (1) continuation.finish() is never called — the stream never terminates, leaking all captured resources. (2) No onTermination handler — when the consumer cancels, the CLLocationManager keeps running. (3) manager is created locally without a retained reference — it will be deallocated immediately after the closure returns, stopping location updates before they begin.

assertions.text: Identifies missing continuation.finish() causes the stream to never terminate
assertions.rationale: Core AsyncStream leak from asyncstream-memory.md

assertions.text: Identifies missing onTermination handler — CLLocationManager keeps running after consumer cancels
assertions.rationale: onTermination for cleanup is specifically prescribed in asyncstream-memory.md

assertions.text: Notes CLLocationManager is created without a retained reference and will be immediately deallocated
assertions.rationale: Object lifetime issue in the AsyncStream initializer closure

evals.name: asyncstream-medium
evals.expected_output: Three issues: (1) The unstructured Task in startConsuming() is never cancelled — it keeps running even after NotificationService deinits, because the stream never finishes. (2) The NotificationCenter observer is never removed — no onTermination handler to call removeObserver when the stream is cancelled. (3) self.continuation = continuation creates a reference cycle: continuation retains the closure which captures self via the weak reference, but self also holds the continuation. Store the observer token and remove it in onTermination.

assertions.text: Identifies the unstructured Task in startConsuming is never cancelled — runs indefinitely
assertions.rationale: Unstructured Task without cancellation is a leak

assertions.text: Identifies missing onTermination to remove the NotificationCenter observer
assertions.rationale: onTermination for observer cleanup is the prescribed pattern from asyncstream-memory.md

assertions.text: Identifies retain cycle between self, continuation, and the observer closure
assertions.rationale: AsyncStream continuation capture creates cycles

assertions.text: Identifies the computed stream property creates a new AsyncStream (and new observer) on every access
assertions.rationale: WITH response identifies this as a distinct fourth bug; WITHOUT misses the repeated-construction issue

assertions.text: Recommends AsyncStream.makeStream(of:) (Swift 5.9+) to separate stream and continuation for proper lifecycle management
assertions.rationale: WITH response uses the modern makeStream API; WITHOUT does not mention this Swift 5.9+ factory method

evals.name: asyncstream-complex
evals.expected_output: Three issues: (1) Regular TaskGroup with Void child tasks still accumulates child task metadata in memory until iterated — for fire-and-forget use withDiscardingTaskGroup, which releases child results immediately. (2) No backpressure: if the stream produces events faster than processEvent consumes them, the group grows without bound — should limit concurrent tasks to activeProcessorCount. (3) If stream is infinite, group.waitForAll() is never reached, but the group still accumulates task overhead. Fix: use withDiscardingTaskGroup and throttle by checking active task count before addTask.

assertions.text: Identifies regular TaskGroup with Void results accumulates child task metadata — recommends withDiscardingTaskGroup
assertions.rationale: withDiscardingTaskGroup is a specific Swift 5.9+ API from asyncstream-memory.md — baseline typically misses this

assertions.text: Identifies missing backpressure — group grows unboundedly if stream is faster than consumers
assertions.rationale: Backpressure is a core AsyncStream concern from skill

assertions.text: Recommends throttling concurrent tasks to activeProcessorCount
assertions.rationale: Skill's specific throttle recommendation for unbounded work

evals.name: task-structured-simple
evals.expected_output: It compiles with a warning (Swift 5) or error (Swift 6). Task.detached strips the caller's @MainActor isolation — the closure runs nonisolated, so assigning self.title (a @MainActor property) from a detached task crosses isolation boundaries. Fix: use Task {} (not Task.detached) to inherit the caller's @MainActor isolation. Task.detached is almost never the right choice when called from an isolated context.

assertions.text: Identifies Task.detached strips @MainActor isolation from the caller
assertions.rationale: Task.detached vs Task {} distinction from advanced-patterns.md

assertions.text: Identifies self.title assignment from detached task is an isolation crossing (warning/error in Swift 6)
assertions.rationale: Concrete consequence of stripping isolation

assertions.text: Recommends Task {} instead of Task.detached to inherit caller's actor
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: Task.detached is rarely correct from an isolated context — baseline often accepts detached as equivalent

assertions.text: Notes Task.detached also strips priority inheritance, task-local values, and cancellation propagation (not just actor isolation)
assertions.rationale: WITH response lists all four things Task.detached strips; WITHOUT only covers actor isolation stripping

evals.name: task-structured-medium
evals.expected_output: Use withThrowingTaskGroup for the dynamic set (B). async let is fixed at compile time — it cannot be used in a loop or when count is unknown at compile time. TaskGroup handles dynamic counts: add one task per postID, collect results with for await. async let (A) is correct for the known pair (user + notifications) — it is simpler for heterogeneous return types and a fixed count. If postIDs could be thousands, also add throttling to cap concurrent tasks at activeProcessorCount.

assertions.text: Recommends TaskGroup (withThrowingTaskGroup) for the dynamic postIDs
assertions.rationale: TaskGroup for dynamic counts vs async let for fixed compile-time counts

assertions.text: Explains async let is fixed at compile time and cannot be used in a loop
assertions.rationale: Key async let limitation from advanced-patterns.md — baseline often suggests async let in loops, which is a compile error

assertions.text: Confirms async let is correct for the fixed pair (user + notifications)
assertions.rationale: async let is the simplest choice for small fixed heterogeneous concurrent work

evals.name: task-structured-complex
evals.expected_output: Adds 50,000 child tasks immediately — each task object is allocated before any work completes, causing OOM. Fix: throttle with a producer-consumer pattern inside the group. Add tasks up to a limit (activeProcessorCount * 2), then use group iteration to wait for one to finish before adding the next. For fire-and-forget tasks, withDiscardingTaskGroup avoids result accumulation overhead. Pattern: use a sliding window — for-loop inside the group.next() iteration to maintain a constant number of in-flight tasks.

assertions.text: Identifies 50,000 tasks allocated simultaneously causes OOM before any work completes
assertions.rationale: TaskGroup OOM from upfront task allocation is explicitly in crash-patterns.md

assertions.text: Recommends throttling with a sliding window — add new task only after one completes
assertions.rationale: Correct TaskGroup throttling pattern from skill

assertions.text: Mentions withDiscardingTaskGroup to avoid result accumulation overhead for Void tasks
assertions.rationale: withDiscardingTaskGroup is the skill-specific optimization — baseline typically only addresses task count

evals.name: cancellation-simple
evals.expected_output: CancellationError is surfaced to the user as an error alert. When a Task is cancelled, fetchData() throws CancellationError — the user sees a confusing 'cancelled' message. Fix: catch CancellationError separately and return silently before the generic catch. Rule: CancellationError should always be handled silently, never shown to users.

assertions.text: Identifies CancellationError is caught by the generic catch and shown to user
assertions.rationale: CancellationError must be caught silently from cancellation-patterns.md

assertions.text: Adds catch is CancellationError { return } before the generic catch
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: CancellationError never surfaces to users — specific pattern from cancellation-patterns.md

evals.name: cancellation-medium
evals.expected_output: The loop has no cooperative cancellation check — it runs to completion even if the parent Task is cancelled. Purely synchronous CPU loops don't yield to the cooperative pool and can't be interrupted. Fix: check Task.isCancelled or call try Task.checkCancellation() periodically (e.g. every 1000 iterations). For very fast inner loops, checking every N iterations is more efficient than every iteration.

assertions.text: Identifies the CPU loop has no cancellation check and will not respond to parent Task cancellation
assertions.rationale: Cooperative cancellation requires explicit checks in synchronous loops

assertions.text: Recommends calling try Task.checkCancellation() or checking Task.isCancelled periodically
assertions.rationale: Correct cooperative cancellation hook from cancellation-patterns.md

assertions.text: Suggests checking every N iterations rather than every iteration for performance
assertions.rationale: Skill's efficiency guidance for hot loops — baseline typically suggests checking every iteration

evals.name: cancellation-complex
evals.expected_output: When the Task is cancelled, Task.sleep throws CancellationError and the function exits — but the server-side upload keeps running under uploadID. Fix: wrap the polling loop in withTaskCancellationHandler { cancelUpload(id: uploadID) } to call a cleanup function when the Task is cancelled. The cancellation handler must be synchronous (non-async) and may be called from any thread — schedule async cleanup via an unstructured Task if needed.

assertions.text: Identifies Task cancellation exits the client but leaves server-side upload running — resource leak
assertions.rationale: External resource cleanup on cancellation from cancellation-patterns.md

assertions.text: Recommends withTaskCancellationHandler to trigger cancelUpload when the Task is cancelled
assertions.rationale: withTaskCancellationHandler is the specific API from skill — baseline may suggest try-catch which misses proactive cleanup

assertions.text: Notes the cancellation handler must be synchronous and may be called from any thread
assertions.rationale: Cancellation handler threading constraint from cancellation-patterns.md — baseline rarely covers this

evals.name: migration-simple
evals.prompt: How do I start migrating an existing multi-target iOS project to Swift 6 strict concurrency without breaking everything at once?
evals.expected_output: Two-phase approach: (1) First set SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY=complete in build settings — this enables all concurrency warnings but does NOT enforce them as errors. This is different from Swift 6 language mode. Fix warnings module-by-module before switching to Swift 6 mode. (2) Migrate bottom-up: start with leaf modules (no internal dependencies), then work toward the app target. One module per PR. Run Thread Sanitizer in CI throughout to catch runtime races the compiler misses.

assertions.text: Distinguishes SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY=complete (warnings) from Swift 6 language mode (errors)
assertions.rationale: This distinction is commonly confused — skill specifically prescribes complete as the first step

assertions.text: Recommends bottom-up migration starting with leaf modules
assertions.rationale: Core migration strategy from migration-strategy.md

assertions.text: Recommends running Thread Sanitizer in CI throughout the migration
assertions.rationale: TSan catches runtime races compiler misses

assertions.text: Describes a three-step progression: targeted -> complete -> Swift 6 language mode (not just complete vs Swift 6)
assertions.rationale: WITH response prescribes the targeted intermediate step; WITHOUT jumps straight from complete to Swift 6 without the targeted phase

assertions.text: Mentions LIBDISPATCH_COOPERATIVE_POOL_STRICT=1 environment variable for exposing cooperative pool deadlocks during migration
assertions.rationale: WITH response includes this debugging tool for migration testing; WITHOUT does not mention it

evals.name: migration-medium
evals.prompt:
Enabling SWIFT_STRICT_CONCURRENCY=complete gives these errors on global state. How should each be fixed?

```swift
// AppGlobals.swift
var currentTheme: AppTheme = .light         // Error: not concurrency-safe
var featureFlags: [String: Bool] = [:]     // Error: not concurrency-safe
let appVersion: String = "2.1.0"           // ?
```
evals.expected_output: Global var with mutable value types is not concurrency-safe under strict concurrency. Three cases: (1) currentTheme: add @MainActor if used only on main thread (UI state), or wrap in an actor for broader access. (2) featureFlags: dictionary mutation is not safe globally — wrap in an actor or use Mutex<[String: Bool]> for synchronous access. (3) appVersion: let constant is already safe — no change needed. Never use nonisolated(unsafe) without documented synchronization guarantees.

assertions.text: Identifies global var causes strict concurrency error (not concurrency-safe)
assertions.rationale: Global mutable state migration from migration-strategy.md

assertions.text: Recommends @MainActor for UI-related global state (currentTheme)
assertions.rationale: Correct isolation for UI-only global state

assertions.text: Recommends actor or Mutex for featureFlags (shared read-write dictionary)
assertions.rationale: Skill decision tree: Mutex for sync access vs actor for async — baseline often only suggests actor

assertions.text: Correctly identifies let constant (appVersion) is already safe — no change needed
assertions.rationale: Discriminating: knows let globals are fine, doesn't over-engineer

evals.name: migration-complex
evals.expected_output: Two strategies for third-party non-Sendable blockers: (1) Short-term: use @preconcurrency import OldAnalyticsSDK to downgrade errors to warnings — allows migration to proceed without immediately fixing the third-party blocker. (2) Long-term: wrap OldSDK.Tracker in a dedicated actor that owns the instance and mediates all access. All callers go through the actor — the non-Sendable type never crosses isolation boundaries directly. Never use @unchecked Sendable on the wrapper.

assertions.text: Recommends wrapping the third-party non-Sendable type in a dedicated actor
assertions.rationale: Actor wrapper for third-party blockers is the skill's prescribed pattern from migration-strategy.md

assertions.text: Recommends @preconcurrency import as a short-term escape hatch to downgrade errors to warnings
assertions.rationale: @preconcurrency import is a Swift-specific migration tool from migration-strategy.md — rarely known without skill

assertions.text: States the non-Sendable instance must never cross isolation boundaries directly — all access through the actor
assertions.rationale: Core containment requirement for non-Sendable third-party types

evals.name: security-concurrency-simple
evals.expected_output: TOCTOU (Time-of-Check/Time-of-Use) at the suspension point. token is checked before await apiCall(), but actor reentrancy allows another caller to modify or nil out token during the suspension. By the time useToken(token!) executes, token may be nil — causing a crash — or may hold a different (expired) value. Fix: capture the token before the suspension point: guard let token else { throw ... }; then use the captured token after the await.

assertions.text: Identifies TOCTOU at the await suspension point — token checked before await, may change after
assertions.rationale: TOCTOU at await is a key security pattern from security-concurrency.md — baseline may only note the force-unwrap crash

assertions.text: Explains actor reentrancy allows state mutation during the suspension
assertions.rationale: Reentrancy is the mechanism enabling the TOCTOU

assertions.text: Recommends capturing the token before the suspension point (guard let token = token else { throw })
assertions.rationale: Correct fix: snapshot state before any await

assertions.text: Recommends re-checking token validity after the suspension point for security-critical scenarios (e.g., guard token == self.token)
assertions.rationale: WITH response provides post-suspension re-check hardening pattern; WITHOUT only captures before suspension without re-check

evals.name: security-concurrency-medium
evals.expected_output: Actor reentrancy token refresh race: After await refreshToken(), isRefreshing is true — but multiple concurrent callers entered makeRequest() before the first refresh completed. Each sees isExpired=true and isRefreshing=false (their snapshot before the first suspension), so each starts a separate refresh. Result: N parallel token refreshes, N token invalidations, cascading 401s. Fix: use Task coalescing — store a Task<String, Error>? for in-flight refresh; new callers await the existing task's value instead of starting a new refresh. Check isRefreshing only after await to handle concurrent entries.

assertions.text: Identifies actor reentrancy causes multiple concurrent callers to each start a refresh
assertions.rationale: Token refresh race is the canonical security-concurrency.md example — requires understanding reentrancy at suspension points

assertions.text: Explains the race: callers check isRefreshing before the first suspension, so all see false
assertions.rationale: Tracing the reentrancy flow through the specific code

assertions.text: Recommends Task coalescing — single in-flight Task<String, Error>? that all callers await
assertions.rationale: Specific coalescing pattern from security-concurrency.md — isRefreshing flag alone is insufficient

assertions.text: Mentions OAuth token rotation (RFC 6749) requiring single-use refresh tokens as a security justification for coalescing
assertions.rationale: WITH response cites RFC 6749 Section 10.4 and token rotation security implications; WITHOUT lacks OAuth protocol-level reasoning

evals.name: security-concurrency-complex
evals.expected_output: Three security issues: (1) Task.detached captures the full credentials object — sensitive data escapes into an unstructured task with unknown lifetime. If the detached task is delayed, credentials may persist in memory longer than needed. Log only credentials.userID (non-sensitive) and avoid capturing the full struct. (2) If the parent Task is cancelled between Keychain read and return, credentials are captured in the detached task with no cleanup path. Use Task {} (structured) to tie the analytics call to the parent's lifetime. (3) The LAContext evaluatePolicy callback has no cancellation path — if the parent Task is cancelled during biometric prompt, the continuation is not resumed, causing a permanent hang. Add a withTaskCancellationHandler to call context.invalidate() and resume the continuation with an error.

assertions.text: Identifies Task.detached captures full sensitive credentials — sensitive data escapes unstructured Task
assertions.rationale: Sensitive data + Task.detached capture is a security-concurrency.md pattern — baseline may miss the sensitivity angle

assertions.text: Recommends logging only non-sensitive fields (userID) and using Task {} instead of Task.detached
assertions.rationale: Minimize sensitive data exposure in unstructured concurrency

assertions.text: Identifies LAContext evaluatePolicy has no cancellation path — parent Task cancellation causes continuation hang
assertions.rationale: Missing cancellation path for biometric continuation is a subtle hang from security-concurrency.md + cancellation-patterns.md

assertions.text: Recommends withTaskCancellationHandler to call context.invalidate() and resume the continuation with an error on cancellation
assertions.rationale: Specific fix combining withTaskCancellationHandler + continuation cancellation — requires skill knowledge

assertions.text: Converts BiometricAuthManager to an actor with Task coalescing to prevent concurrent authentication flows
assertions.rationale: WITH response converts class to actor with stored authTask for coalescing; WITHOUT keeps it as a plain class without serialization
